guest_role: General Partner at Kleiner Perkins; co-founder of Packaged (former CTO of Flipboard)
assertiveness_why: Harry asks prepared, multi-sentence questions and occasionally plants his own thesis for Eric to confirm, but he never contests or pressure-tests Eric's answers, mostly receiving and agreeing.
question_style: Mostly open, well-researched questions with some leading framing where Harry states his own view (e.g. on incumbents' M&A advantage, on the need to stay 'ahead of the curve') and asks Eric to agree; no interruptions, no real pushback, and Eric readily affirms each framing.
tone: Warm, matey, and fan-like — heavy praise of Kleiner Perkins and Eric's background, some self-deprecating banter ('why aren't you throwing yourself off a bridge'), high energy and enthusiasm throughout.
depth: Mostly strategic/thesis-level rather than technical: covers consumer investing lulls, distribution/incumbent dynamics, transactional vs ad business models, and KP's internal data-tooling edge, but doesn't probe technical mechanics of the tools or metrics in detail.
harry_quotes: Are we in a bit of a consumer lull in your eyes?
Okay. So Eric, why aren't you throwing yourself off a bridge?
What would you most like to change in the world of VC?
learnings: Eric argues consumer breakout hits have stalled since Snapchat (2011) mainly because free distribution channels (SEO, App Store, social platforms) are now saturated and pay-to-play.

Today's tech incumbents grew up during the disruption era themselves, so they recognize threats early and respond via acquisition or direct copying, making them far harder to blindside than prior-generation giants like IBM or Yahoo.
Commerce and content are merging — apps like OfferUp show browsing-driven marketplaces generating session times rivaling media and social apps.
Falling payment friction (Apple Pay, Venmo, Stripe) is reviving transactional business models as a better fit for early-stage startups than ad-based models, which require massive scale to work.
Kleiner Perkins invests in proprietary internal data tooling (app store signals, transaction data trends) to find an investing edge, similar to firms like Signal Fire, Goodwater, and Google Ventures.

Feng views consumer investing as binary — once product-market fit is proven, entry price barely matters, favoring a barbell strategy of investing either very early or after clear inflection, regardless of valuation.
notable: Includes an extended personal-history segment about Feng meeting Al Gore through Kleiner Perkins, which led to him joining the firm.
